messages.author: EricT
messages.text: Hi, sorry I'm late to the game guys. Just found discord, but I can't figure out where to post here. This may not suit MeshCore's needs, but it's an idea to perhaps consider along with the rest in this communal stew. Regarding "path" spam, etc, causing denial of service through amplification. Once upon a time I designed a distributed, trust-based, non-hierarchical private- or publicly-networked TCP/IP system (AEON: Ad-hoc Emergency Online Network), backed of necessity by self-signed certificates (similar to MeshCore's private/public key system). Like Meshtastic, all nodes were also repeaters, but there was a limit on message spread. Breach of trust in this kind of network is inevitable and needs to be mitigated. Anyone could join, and anyone could moderate. Individual users could decide by ticking a box which of their contacts was trusted as a moderator. And anyone could send out block notices or subsequent block revocation notices. If you trusted the issuer, then you would refuse to forward messages from the offending user and also repeat the block or revocation itself. This limits an offender's audience reach and lessens traffic. If you hadn't chosen to trust the issuer, you would not act on the block or revocation, but you would repeat it. Blocks and revocations could be composed like other messages, so as to be human-readable, and contain very brief reasoning. As I say: just a thought.
messages.content_html: Hi, sorry I'm late to the game guys. Just found discord, but I can't figure out where to post here. This may not suit MeshCore's needs, but it's an idea to perhaps consider along with the rest in this communal stew. Regarding "path" spam, etc, causing denial of service through amplification. Once upon a time I designed a distributed, trust-based, non-hierarchical private- or publicly-networked TCP/IP system (AEON: Ad-hoc Emergency Online Network), backed of necessity by self-signed certificates (similar to MeshCore's private/public key system). Like Meshtastic, all nodes were also repeaters, but there was a limit on message spread. Breach of trust in this kind of network is inevitable and needs to be mitigated. Anyone could join, and anyone could moderate. Individual users could decide by ticking a box which of their contacts was trusted as a moderator. And anyone could send out block notices or subsequent block revocation notices. If you trusted the issuer, then you would refuse to forward messages from the offending user and also repeat the block or revocation itself. This limits an offender's audience reach and lessens traffic. If you hadn't chosen to trust the issuer, you would not act on the block or revocation, but you would repeat it. Blocks and revocations could be composed like other messages, so as to be human-readable, and contain very brief reasoning. As I say: just a thought.

messages.author: stretch ZL3XL
messages.text: Hi @EricT came here from your FB post.
messages.content_html: Hi @EricT came here from your FB post.

messages.author: EricT
messages.text: Wow! Many thanks, much appreciated ZL3XL.
messages.content_html: Wow! Many thanks, much appreciated ZL3XL.
messages.reactions_html: 1
messages.reply_html:
stretch ZL3XL
This is what we're seeing in New Zealand. Repeater flood advert spamming/spoofing. One malicious actor is sending a new repeater flood advert, changing key name, location, and sending again. Sometimes as often as every few seconds. We've narrowed it down to roughly where it's coming from, based on which repeaters hear directly (then flood repeat) the spam, and which legit repeaters have the spam repeaters in their neighbours list. It seemed to be a dig at those of us that are proponents of moving to 2-byte, and are actively trying to manage/deconflict 1st-byte prefix use as new users come online. We don't know what the long-term solution is, but the few of us who are thinking, are thinking of: A) Blacklist-by-default of new Repeaters until a Companion vouches for the new Repeater. Do not acknowledge/repeater anything from the untrusted repeater until verified. The problem is, the spammer could just spam Companion flood adverts instead, and you can't blacklist-by-default new Companions, and that's every new, legitimate users. The best mitigation we have at the moment is to advise everyone to turn off the auto-add feature in the Discover Contacts settings. We learned that after the first incident a few weeks ago, where 600+ new repeaters appeared over an hour or so, wiping out most people's contacts via 'delete oldest first'. We also turn off repeating for the affected zero-hop repeaters for a period of time.

messages.author: EricT
messages.text: Keys can be changed. Does Meshcore have access to device serial numbers as a fixed ID?
messages.content_html: Keys can be changed. Does Meshcore have access to device serial numbers as a fixed ID?
messages.reply_html:
stretch ZL3XL
Hi @EricT came here from your FB post.

messages.author: stretch ZL3XL
messages.text: No - to my knowledge there is no hard-coded MAC address equivalent.
messages.content_html: No - to my knowledge there is no hard-coded MAC address equivalent.
